task: I’ve got a workday coming up where I may need to keep moving if the power or internet cuts out for a while. What’s a sensible way to set up my materials and workspace so I can shift to a different spot and keep going without losing track of what I was doing?

turns.agent_response:
A good setup is to make it easy to “pause and resume” anywhere.

**Before the workday**
- Keep **one master list** of tasks for the day, with the next action on each item.
- Save everything in **cloud-synced folders** if possible, and make sure key files are available **offline** too.
- Put the most important docs in a **single folder** so you’re not hunting for them.
- Have a **portable charger / laptop battery** and, if relevant, a phone hotspot ready.
- If you use notes often, keep a **paper notebook or one simple text note** for quick status updates.

**Set up your workspace so it’s easy to move**
- Use a **small “grab-and-go” kit**: laptop, charger, headphones, notebook, pen, any adapters.
- Keep cables and accessories in one pouch so you can relocate quickly.
- If you might switch spots, avoid spreading materials across multiple surfaces.
- Leave a **clear “current work” note** open or written down:  
  - what you were doing  
  - what’s next  
  - any blockers  
  - where the latest file version is saved

**When the connection or power drops**
- Save immediately.
- Write a 1–2 line checkpoint: “Stopped at X; next step is Y.”
- Switch to offline tasks if you can: drafting, reviewing, outlining, organizing files.
- If you move locations, bring only the grab-and-go kit and your checkpoint note.

**A simple system that works well**
Use this format in a note:
- **Task:**  
- **Current step:**  
- **Next step:**  
- **File/location:**  
- **Waiting on:**  

If you want, I can help you build a **very short outage-proof checklist** tailored to the kind of work you’ll be doing.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty solid, but I’d want it tighter and more practical for actual use. The checklist idea is good. I’d add one thing: keep a printed copy of the day’s key terms or reference notes, not just the files. When the connection goes bad, that’s usually what slows me down.

Also, I’d want a little more on version control. Like: one working file, one backup copy, and a clear name for the latest version. That avoids the stupid confusion later.

If you want to make it useful for me, give me a one-page setup I can follow before I start — what stays on the desk, what goes in the bag, and what gets written on the paper note before I move.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter, practical one-page setup you can use before you start.

## Before you begin: set up for moving fast

### Keep on the desk
- Laptop
- Charger
- Mouse/headphones if you use them
- One notebook + pen
- Printed **key terms / reference notes**
- Current task list or checklist

### Put in the bag
- Laptop charger / power bank
- Phone charger
- Any adapters or dongles
- Backup internet option if you have one
- The printed reference sheet
- The paper note you’ll update as you go

### File setup
- **One working file** = the file you’re actively editing
- **One backup copy** = saved separately
- Use a clear name like:  
  `ProjectName_current`  
  or  
  `ProjectName_2026-04-29_v3`
- Make sure autosave and sync are on before you disconnect

## What to write on the paper note before you move
Keep it short and readable:

- **What I’m working on:**
- **Current file/version:**
- **Next step:**
- **What’s done:**
- **What’s blocked:**
- **Offline reference needed:**

## Quick rule for moving
Before you leave the spot:
1. Save the file
2. Update the version name if needed
3. Write the 1-minute paper note
4. Pack the bag items
5. Move with the current note and printed reference sheet

## Best practice for outages
- Do anything that can be done offline first
- Keep the printed reference notes close by
- If the connection drops, switch to the paper note and continue from the **next step** line

If you want, I can turn this into a **clean printable checklist** with checkboxes and a tiny “done / pending / blocked” section.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s closer. Still a little generic, but usable.

What I’d want added is the actual version rule — not just “name it clearly.” Something like:
- working file only
- backup copy saved separately
- don’t overwrite until the new version is confirmed saved

And the paper note should have one more line for **where the latest copy is**. That’s the thing people forget when they’re bouncing around.

So, before I move, I’d want:
- current file name
- latest saved location
- next step
- anything I’m waiting on
- printed terms/reference sheet in the bag

If you make the printable checklist, keep it plain. No fluff. Just checkboxes and short lines.

turns.agent_response:
Got it. Here’s a plain, practical version you can actually use.

## Outage-proof work setup

### Keep on the desk
- [ ] Laptop
- [ ] Charger
- [ ] Mouse / headphones
- [ ] Notebook + pen
- [ ] Printed key terms / reference sheet
- [ ] Current task list

### Put in the bag
- [ ] Charger / power bank
- [ ] Phone charger
- [ ] Adapters / dongles
- [ ] Backup internet option, if you have one
- [ ] Printed key terms / reference sheet
- [ ] Paper note for status updates

### File rule
- [ ] One working file only
- [ ] One backup copy saved separately
- [ ] Do not overwrite the old version until the new one is confirmed saved
- [ ] Latest version has a clear name

Example:
- `ProjectName_working`
- `ProjectName_backup`
- `ProjectName_2026-04-29_v3`

### Before you move, write this on paper
- [ ] Current file name:
- [ ] Latest saved location:
- [ ] Next step:
- [ ] Waiting on:
- [ ] Printed terms/reference sheet packed:

### Move rule
- [ ] Save the file
- [ ] Confirm the new version saved
- [ ] Update the paper note
- [ ] Pack the reference sheet
- [ ] Move with the bag and note

### If power or internet drops
- [ ] Stop and save
- [ ] Check the paper note
- [ ] Work from printed references if needed
- [ ] Resume at the next step

If you want, I can turn this into a **true one-page printable version** with tighter spacing and checkbox formatting.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s basically it. Close enough to use, but I’d tighten the file rule one more notch.

I’d want the note to say:
- current file name
- latest saved location
- next step
- waiting on
- printed terms/reference sheet packed

And the version rule should be dead simple:
- working file stays in use
- backup copy stays separate
- don’t touch the old one until the new one is confirmed saved

That part matters. I’ve had enough little file mix-ups to know the clean rule saves time later.

If you make the printable version, keep it plain and compact. Checkbox lines only. No extra explanation.

turns.agent_response:
## Outage-proof work checklist

### On desk
- [ ] Laptop

- [ ] Mouse / headphones

### In bag

- [ ] Printed terms / reference sheet
- [ ] Paper note

### File rule
- [ ] Working file stays in use
- [ ] Backup copy stays separate
- [ ] Don’t touch the old one until the new one is confirmed saved

### Before moving, write on paper
- [ ] Current file name:
- [ ] Latest saved location:
- [ ] Next step:
- [ ] Waiting on:
- [ ] Printed terms/reference sheet packed:

### Before you move
- [ ] Save file
- [ ] Confirm new version saved
- [ ] Update paper note
- [ ] Pack reference sheet
- [ ] Move with bag and note

### If power or internet drops
- [ ] Save
- [ ] Check paper note
- [ ] Use printed references
- [ ] Resume at next step

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that does it. Plain enough to use, and the file rule is finally stated the way I’d actually write it down. The paper note items are right too — especially the latest saved location. That’s the part that saves you later when you’re moving around and don’t want to stop and think.

This is usable as-is.
